abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly rely on instruction tuning—the process of finetuning on a diverse range of tasks—to align their behavior with human intent. However, human-curated instruction data is often limited in volume, lacking in diversity, or costly to produce. We introduce SELF-INSTRUCT, a semi-automated framework for improving the instruction-following capabilities of pretrained language models by leveraging their own generation power. Specifically, our pipeline uses a small set of seed human-written instructions to prompt the model to generate new tasks and corresponding input-output instances. By applying rigorous filtering to ensure quality and diversity, the model iteratively bootstraps its own training data. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that SELF-INSTRUCT significantly enhances the performance of a vanilla GPT-3 model, narrowing the gap with state-of-the-art models that were trained on private, human-annotated datasets. We release our synthetic dataset of 52,000 instructions to facilitate further research into the self-alignment of large-scale models.
